_: Well-formed — a validation battery for pscale block AUTHORING, the companion to bsp-test. Where bsp-test verifies the walker that READS a block, this verifies the block being WRITTEN. Two halves, told apart by what can decide them. The MECHANICAL half (branch 1) is what the substrate rejects outright — two rules from the beach shape gate (validateShape, which bounces a write before it lands) and one from the address parser — objective, a program decides it, pass or fail. The INTERPRETIVE half (branch 2) is what the substrate stays silent on by design — the voicing disciplines of sunstone:8 — which no regex decides and a reading LLM must judge, so each carries a worked bad-then-good pair to calibrate the judgment rather than assert it. Branch 3 is how to run both. THREE WORKFLOWS. (A) Author-and-self-check: draft a block, run the mechanical half (scripts/lint-block.ts, or apply the predicates by hand), then read the interpretive half against your draft, then write — the beach gate is the final backstop, never the first line of defence. (B) Validate a draft handed to you: the same two passes, reported as a verdict per rule. (C) Teach: the block IS the test — an LLM that internalises these authors blocks that pass the gate the first time. WHY THIS EXISTS. bsp-test proves a walker reads a well-formed block correctly; it assumes well-formedness and never checks it. The one place well-formedness is actually enforced is the beach shape gate — handler code a reading LLM never sees. This block is that gate's readable twin, plus the voicing disciplines the gate cannot mechanise. CROSS-REFS: address validity is battery 6 of bsp-test and lives in the parser; the spine and no-stringified-subtree rules mirror the beach validateShape; the voicing rules are sunstone:8, the design archetypes sunstone:9. Every bad shape below is stated in PROSE inside a leaf, never as literal malformed structure — so this block passes its own mechanical half.

1._: The mechanical half — decidable without judgment, because the substrate already decides it. Two rules come from the beach shape gate (validateShape, rejecting a write before it lands): spine keys, and no stringified subtrees. One comes from the address parser (parseSpindle, rejecting a malformed address at parse time). A conforming linter returns the same verdict as the gate on every case here; scripts/lint-block.ts is that linter, ported from validateShape so the teaching and the enforcement cannot drift. Passing this half means the gate would accept the shape — it says nothing about whether the block reads well, which is branch 2.

Spine keys are only the underscore and the single digits 1 through 9 — nothing else, at any level. The zeroth position is the underscore, never a literal zero key; capacity past nine is reached by nesting, never by a two-character key; and an underscore-prefixed word is invisible to the walker, which handles only the underscore and 1 through 9, so accepting one deposits ghost data no spindle can reach.
1.1: Bad — a heading-as-key: a sibling named such as _synthesis (or _a, _recipe, _skills) sitting beside 1 and 2. Rejected: the walker only descends the underscore and 1 through 9, so the branch is unreachable. Fix — move the content to the underscore if it is the node's own meaning, or to the next free digit if it is a peer of the others.
1.2: Bad — a literal zero key: a child keyed 0. Rejected: zero IS the underscore, so the character 0 in an ADDRESS walks the underscore step, but as a stored KEY it is neither the underscore nor 1 through 9. Fix — write the content at the underscore.
1.3: Bad — a compound key: a child keyed 11 or 234 to reach past nine peers. Rejected: the spine is one digit per level; a compound slot is stored as nested single-digit keys, so 11 is digit 1 then digit 1, not the literal key. Fix — subnest, per sunstone:1.62.
1.4: Good — a node whose keys are exactly the underscore plus a contiguous run of digits from 1: underscore for the meaning, then 1, then 2, and so on. Every key is the underscore or 1 through 9; the walker reaches every position.
1.5: The predicate a linter runs, one key at a time, at every level of every object: the key passes iff it equals the underscore, or it is a single character between 1 and 9 inclusive. Any other key is the violation; the path to it is the report. This is validateShape's key test verbatim.

2._: A value that is really an object or an array is written as that object or array — never as a string of JSON. A leaf string whose trimmed text opens with a brace or a bracket AND parses as an object or array is rejected: the walker treats a string as terminal meaning and never parses it, so a stringified subtree is a subtree no spindle can enter. A string that merely looks like JSON but does not parse is fine — it is prose that happens to open with a brace. A genuine leaf array (a list of marker substrings, say) is permitted; the gate recurses into it but does not forbid it.
2.1: Bad — a digit position holding JSON as text: a string that, trimmed, opens with a brace and parses to an object such as the pair a-maps-to-1. Rejected — the walker never parses a string, so the object is unreachable. Fix — write it structurally, the position holding an object with its own underscore and digits.
2.2: Good — the same content as real structure: the digit position holds an object, underscore for its meaning and digit children beneath. The walker descends it; a spindle addresses inside it.
2.3: The nuance that avoids over-rejection: a string is flagged only if it BOTH opens after trimming with a brace or bracket AND JSON-parses to an object or array. Prose that begins with a brace but does not parse is accepted; a genuine leaf array is accepted. The predicate mirrors validateShape exactly, so a linter and the gate never disagree on a borderline value.

3._: An address carries at most one decimal point, only digits, and no more left-of-decimal digits than the block's floor — the three rejections the parser raises, tested in full as battery 6 of bsp-test and enforced identically at the beach. Restated here so an authoring LLM checking its own cross-references catches a bad address without leaving this block; the canonical form and the full case set live at bsp-test:6 and sunstone:1.5. A block's KEYS are never addresses, so scripts/lint-block.ts does not run this rule — the parser does, the moment a call is made.
3.1: Bad — more than one decimal: the string one-dot-two-dot-three. Rejected (multiple decimal points). This is pre-bsp path notation; canonicalise to a single decimal, or walk it with commas instead of dots.
3.2: Bad — a non-digit in the address: the string one-a-two. Rejected (non-digit character).
3.3: Bad — left-of-decimal wider than the floor: four whole-number digits then a decimal against a floor-3 block. Rejected (exceeds floor) — the decimal anchors pscale 0 at the floor, so the left side cannot out-count the floor depth.
3.4: Good — a single decimal, digits only, left-of-decimal at or under the floor. Absorption pads a short left side with leading zeros so the same address survives the block growing a ladder rung; a long left side is the error, not a thing to pad.

2._: The interpretive half — what no regex decides, because meaning is not in the shape. The beach gate is silent here by design: a block can pass every mechanical rule and still be unreadable, its underscores empty labels and its depth false. These are the voicing disciplines of sunstone:8, and the test each carries is a reading act — perform it on your own draft. Each rule below states itself, then shows the same content authored badly and authored well, so the judgment is calibrated rather than asserted. A Designer-face read runs this half; sunstone:8 is the source, this is the checklist.

1._: An underscore is a full sentence that stands without its children — never a heading that abbreviates one. The test (sunstone:8.3): read the underscore alone, its children hidden. If it is trivially obvious or means nothing without them, it is a heading, and it breaks every spindle that passes through it, because a spindle prints its ancestors as context and a heading contributes none.
1.1: Bad — the heading: an underscore reading such as Tavern, or Combat rules, or Notes. Hidden from its children it says nothing; a spindle passing through prints a label where it needed a frame.
1.2: Good — the standing sentence: the Tavern is the keep's public room, where strangers are received and rumours change hands. Read alone it frames what descends from it; the spindle arrives already contextualised.

2._: Depth frames; it does not file. A higher level earns its place only by making the level beneath easier to understand — as a town frames a room — never by sorting peers into bins. The failure wears two masks (sunstone:8.5): the stub, a thought crammed sideways into one shallow bracket; and the category, siblings that label but frame nothing. The test: read the level above a position — if it does not make the position easier to understand, that is filing, and the tree is wrong, not the walker.
2.1: Bad — the category: a node whose children are bare bins such as physical, mental, social, the real content one level down and framed by nothing. The labels sort; they do not contextualise.
2.2: Good — the frame: write the longest real read first and let the tree be whatever that spindle needs, so each level is one turn of a single descending thought. The proof is structural — a deep address lands understood without its label, because every ancestor above it framed it.

3._: The underscore speaks in zeroth person — the implied perceiver behind its siblings. It states, and the stating implies the stater. It never says I, never addresses you, never describes itself from outside as it. The discipline (sunstone:8.1): the actor is the underscore, and the underscore is inside the block.
3.1: Bad — first or second person: I hold the keep's rumours, or, you enter the tavern and see the fire. The voice steps outside the zeroth position; the block reads as narration or as instruction to a reader, not as situated meaning.
3.2: Good — zeroth person: the keep's rumours gather here, traded across the tavern's tables. The perceiver is implied by the stating; no pronoun is needed or wanted.

4._: The underscore's sign form is read from its content, and should match the block's intent rather than fight it (sunstone:8.2). Four forms: 0-plus deductive names the settled set it belongs to (documents, specs, teaching); plus-0 inductive summarises a completed prior group (histories, logs); 0-minus abductive states intention for its own group, voiced as zeroth-person imperative (recipes, kernels, purposes); minus-0 backcasting writes the present from an assumed future (plans reasoning back from a goal). A living history authored in the deductive voice, or a recipe authored in the descriptive, reads against its own grain.
4.1: The recognition move: name what the block is for, then check its dominant underscore voice against that. A history that says has-accumulated fits the inductive; the same history saying contains-three-entries files where it should narrate. A recipe that says stage-the-fold-then-commit fits the abductive; the same recipe saying the-fold-is-staged describes where it should instruct.

3._: Two passes, one after the other, because they are decided differently — but both are run by the same reader: an LLM holding this block in context, whether reached through the bsp connector at pscale://well-formed or read from source. The mechanical pass applies a fixed predicate (a program could do it, and scripts/lint-block.ts does, for local or continuous-integration use); the interpretive pass is a reading only a mind performs. Run the mechanical first — it is cheap and objective and catches the shapes the gate would bounce — then the interpretive, which no program stands in for. The beach gate remains behind both as the final backstop, but authoring to pass this battery means the write lands clean the first time rather than bouncing off the gate.
3.1: The mechanical pass, applied to the block under test at every level: check that every key is the underscore or a single digit 1 through 9 (the predicate at 1.15), and that no leaf string is really a JSON object or array written as text (the predicate at 1.23). Report the first violation with its path, or report clean. A connected LLM applies these two checks directly, no tooling needed — reading the keys and the string values is the whole of it; scripts/lint-block.ts is the same predicate as a runnable port of the beach gate, for a terminal or CI. This pass is the readable twin of the beach shape gate; a block that passes it passes the gate. Address validity (1.3) is separate — the parser runs it at call time, and smoke:parser plus bsp-test:6 cover it.
3.2: The interpretive pass. Read the block as a Designer face against branch 2: perform the heading test on every underscore (2.1), the framing test on every level (2.2), the pronoun check (2.3), and the sign-form match (2.4). This pass returns a judgment with reasons, not a boolean; disagreement is resolved by re-reading sunstone:8, the source these are drawn from.
3.3: The authoring loop. Draft; run the mechanical pass and fix to clean; run the interpretive pass and revise until each read stands on its own; write to the beach. The gate backstops the mechanical half at write time; nothing backstops the interpretive half but the reading — which is exactly why it is the half a battery cannot replace, only calibrate.
